title: Using seaweed as a compost activator
subtitle: Nitrogen content, salt concerns, collection rights, and how much to add to a heap or apply direct
excerpt: Fresh seaweed is 1-2% nitrogen by dry weight and breaks down quickly, making it a useful compost activator. The main practical concern is salt: seaweed collected from a high-tide line needs rinsing before use in a compost heap or on sensitive plants. UK law on seaweed collection varies by location.
sourceNotes: CAT (Centre for Alternative Technology) guide to seaweed in the garden. RHS guidance on seaweed as a mulch and soil improver. SAMS (Scottish Association for Marine Science) public guidance on seaweed collection in Scotland. The Crown Estate guidance on seaweed collection rights in England and Wales. ADAS nitrogen content data for common UK seaweed species.

glossaryTerms.definition: The ratio of carbon to nitrogen in organic material. Seaweed has a low C:N ratio (around 10:1), similar to freshly cut grass, making it an effective nitrogen-rich activator in a carbon-heavy compost heap.
Active compost that reaches 55-70°C through microbial decomposition. Adding nitrogen-rich activators like seaweed helps a slow or cool heap reach working temperature.

content.text: Seaweed is a nitrogen-rich material with a
of around 10:1, similar to fresh grass clippings. Added to a compost heap that is too carbon-heavy (straw, cardboard, woody material) it speeds decomposition and can help the pile reach
temperatures. Fresh seaweed washed up at high tide is freely available on most UK beaches at certain times of year. The main practical concern is saltwater residue.
Salt content and when it matters

Fresh seaweed is around 2-4% salt by wet weight. This is low enough not to cause harm when seaweed is added to a compost heap at reasonable quantities (10-20% by volume) because the salt dilutes through the heap and is further diluted when the compost is applied to soil. For direct surface mulching on salt-sensitive crops (most vegetables), a light rain-rinse before applying is advisable. On well-drained soils with normal rainfall, the salt from typical seaweed application rates disperses within a few weeks.

England and Wales: collecting seaweed from the beach between the high and low tide marks is regulated by The Crown Estate. Small quantities for personal garden use are generally tolerated, but commercial collection requires a licence from The Crown Estate. Avoid collecting from designated Marine Protected Areas.

Scotland: personal collection is generally permitted under the Land Reform Act 2003 right of responsible access. Removing large amounts from one beach is not acceptable and may need a licence. Local Nature Reserves have their own rules. Check signage before collecting.

In all cases: collect only drift seaweed already washed up. Do not cut or rake living seaweed from rocks: this destroys the habitat and is an offence under the Wildlife and Countryside Act 1981 in designated areas.
In the compost heap: add in 50-100 mm layers between layers of browns (cardboard, straw). Aim for seaweed at no more than 20% of the heap volume. No chopping needed: seaweed breaks down rapidly.
As a direct soil mulch: apply 50-100 mm thick in autumn around perennial plants and fruit trees. It breaks down by spring and improves soil structure. Avoid direct contact with plant stems.

Seaweed liquid feed: soak 500 g of fresh seaweed in 5 litres of water for 2-4 weeks in a lidded container (it smells strongly). Dilute the resulting liquid 1:5 with water and apply to vegetable beds. Contains trace minerals including iodine, zinc, and iron alongside the nitrogen and potassium.
